summary: DeVault reflects on his first year working full-time on self-directed free software projects, presenting an honest account of both the rewards and costs. He describes the fulfillment of working on his life's passions with greater depth and breadth, but also the overwhelming volume of work, the loss of anonymity, and the toll on his personal life. He discusses the mixed effects of minor fame — from genuine praise to hostile strangers to bizarre unsolicited messages — and the difficulty of never entering a community as an unknown. Despite unhealthy coping mechanisms and an endless task list, he concludes that writing his own code on his own terms is the most rewarding work he's ever done.
key_points: Full-time FOSS work enables deeper, more careful work across a broader range of projects, but the sheer volume of responsibilities is overwhelming
Minor fame brings a mix of genuine appreciation, unwelcome attention from troubled individuals, and outright hostility from strangers who've never met him
The loss of anonymity means he can never join a new community on equal terms — he often considers creating an alias just to experience that privilege again
Personal life suffers dramatically: no exercise, no dating life, dependence on delivery food, caffeine addiction, and unhealthy coping mechanisms
Despite everything, writing his own code on his own terms is deeply fulfilling — working for others was always an emotional drain, and side projects were his escape long before going full-time
money_quotes: I now feel at once happier, busier, more fulfilled, more engaged, more stressed, and more depressed than I have at any other point in my life.
Often I think about making an alias just to enjoy the privilege of anonymity again.
Thanks to FOSS, I have met an amazing number of kind, talented, generous people.
Writing code for someone else has always been a huge drain on my emotional well-being.
The list of blog posts I want to write is well over 100 entries long.

When I'm in the zone, writing lots of code for a project I'm interested in, knowing it's going to have a meaningful impact on my users, knowing that it's being written under my terms, it's the most rewarding work I've ever done.
It's both terrible and wonderful and very busy, very goddamn busy.
key_insight: Full-time FOSS work is simultaneously the most fulfilling and most overwhelming experience possible — the freedom to write your own code on your own terms comes at the cost of anonymity, personal health, and an ever-growing mountain of responsibilities.
